title: Coconut Chutney

ingredients.title: Ingredients (makes ~1 cup)
items: Roasted chana dal (dalia / bengal gram) – 2 tbsp
Fresh coriander leaves (with tender stems) – 2 tbsp
Chilled water – ½ cup (as needed, for smooth blending)

ingredients.title: Tempering (Tadka)
items: Coconut oil (or sunflower oil) – 1 tbsp (coconut oil gives authentic flavor)
Urad dal (split black gram) – ½ tsp

instruction.title: 1. Prepare the Base Chutney
steps: In a blender, add grated coconut, roasted chana dal, green chilies, ginger, coriander, and salt.
Add ice-cold water little by little and grind to a smooth, creamy chutney. (The cold water prevents heat from the blender altering the coconut’s freshness.)

instruction.title: 2. Adjust Consistency
steps: Chutney should be slightly thick but pourable. Adjust with more chilled water if needed.

instruction.title: 3. Tempering (Tadka)
steps: Heat coconut oil in a small pan.
Add mustard seeds; let them splutter.
Add urad dal and fry until golden.
Add dried red chili, curry leaves, and a pinch of hing. Fry for 10–15 seconds until aromatic.
Pour the hot tempering immediately over the chutney and mix.

chefTips: Use fresh coconut – frozen can be used, but thaw and rinse with warm water first for a fresher taste.
Balance flavors – coriander adds brightness, ginger adds depth, and roasted chana dal gives body.
Keep it chilled – restaurants often keep chutneys cold to prevent fermentation and to keep the color bright.
Oil choice – authentic Kerala/Tamil Nadu restaurants use coconut oil, while Karnataka-style chutney may use sunflower or groundnut oil.
Serve with – idli, dosa, vada, uttapam, or even as a spread for South Indian sandwiches.
